title: Grímnismál
stanzas: Now shall I speak of the great Aesir,
And all of the hosts of the gods so high,
From the high seat of Hlidskialf I can see,
All the worlds that spread far below.
Of Yggdrasil the ash I speak,
The tree so high that it holds all things,
Its branches spread out over heaven,
Its roots lie deep in the earth below.
That tree shall wither and fall at last,
When the Fimbul winter comes to pass,
But new it shall grow from the sea once more,
When the world has washed all things away.
Asgard the home of the gods doth lie,
In the sky so fair and the gold so bright,
There Odin dwells in his hall so grand,
With Frigg at his side in majesty.
Valhalla waits for the slain in battle,
Where Odin's chosen do feast all day,
And Valkyries serve them the mead of mead,
While they wait for Ragnarok's final day.
The golden roof of the hall doth shine,
With treasures of gold and silver bright,
The walls of the hall are made of spears,
And benches bound with bands of gold.
The bridge Bifrost spans the sky,
From Midgard earth to Asgard high,
It burns like fire in the rainbow's light,
Where Heimdall guards both day and night.
Five hundred halls stand in Asgard high,
Each one fairer than the last,
Where the gods do dwell in power great,
And the Aesir and Vanir unite in peace.
In Muspelheim the fire burns bright,
Where Surtr keeps his watch and ward,
And Niflheim is cold and dark,
Where Nidhog gnaws at the roots below.
Midgard is the home of men so bold,
Where the days pass swift and the nights grow long,
And Svartalfheim dwells beneath the earth,
Where the dwarves do forge their gold and stone.
The sun and moon in their courses run,
Around the great wheel of the sky,
Pursued by wolves that ever hunt,
But never catch their golden prey.
The stars appear in the heaven high,
Set by the gods in ancient days,
Each one fixed in its proper place,
To guide the wanderer through the night.
The wind blows from the four corners far,
From Niflheim to Muspel's flame,
And in the great tree's branches high,
An eagle sits and watches all.
The serpent lies at the roots of the tree,
Gnawing the bark and tearing the boughs,
And between them runs a squirrel swift,
Bringing tidings of hatred dire.
At Ragnarok shall the great tree shake,
And the Fimbul winter come to pass,
The sun and moon shall be swallowed whole,
By the wolves that have hunted them so long.
